It makes me a little longer than I expected. OK, guys. So I want to welcome everyone to Total Breakthrough Training. This is Master Unit 4. We're going to be talking about Tony's work with Kristen and Frank Alioto, and also presenting some strategies that we've developed that will help you understand this intervention here. We're going to start off with a strategy and a dynamic. And that's pretty universal, even though that it's a very specific situation. And you'll see this used in Tony, where he works with Frank and Kristen in that short intervention. Even though Frank is a paraplegic and they have a very specific type of situation, the underlying relationship dynamic is universal. And we call this a reaction cycle. It's a pattern of interaction between two people and a very emotional disconnect between them and a division where they feel that they can't meet each other's needs. So it's really common when you're working with a couple that there's an emotion that one member gets, or a way that they express themselves, or something they say that throws the other member off their game into a pattern of helplessness. And this helplessness creates pain for both people. So there's three basic steps. In step one, the person A in the couple, they express themselves. And this can be a man or a woman in a way that's very emotional, very intense, and where they're upset. So they might complain, they might curse, they might show their frustration. Now, partner B reacts emotionally and takes it personally, basically. So people react in different ways to this. Partner B can react with compassion or patience or care, and they can roll up their sleeves and try to help their partner. And that would be a very direct way of meeting partner A's needs, and the reaction cycle would be cut short, and there wouldn't be a problem. We wouldn't be talking about it, right? Now, we're talking about situations where partner B reacts to their partner's emotion with helplessness, with anger, with annoyance, with disconnecting, with guilt, or with shame. And when people... I would also add that as people, especially if we are not used to our anger, and then we enter being parents or whatever situation that brings up this new emotion, to be able to feel okay with the way your anger is, that it's yours, that you can actually think about it and get to know it as a friend, and that it's not someone else's anger, it's not a generic version of anger, it's yours, and she can be your helper, and she can come out in different volumes for different purposes, she can have a higher purpose. I know I had the same thing as a mother, I really was not an angry person at all, and for the first time when I had several young children, I found myself, I actually went to a homeopath who gave me a type of homeopathy to trigger my anger. Oh wow. Yeah, and I suddenly got to know my anger, and it was not other people's anger. I think I had had a big fear, I had a fear of being a judging, harsh-sounding mother. I had that. So I needed to get to know that my anger was there for a reason and had a purpose, and it wasn't anyone else's from my past or in my present, I needed it to be my own. Hmm. I used to have a daughter that when she was about kindergarten would have a meltdown every day after school around 1 p.m. It was like clockwork.
